answers.question: How do I build a city?
answers.response:
- What it costs: Pay 3 ore and 2 grain (resource cards).

- When you can do it: During your turn’s building phase, after rolling the dice (you may build multiple things in any order as long as you can pay).

- How to place it:
  - You must upgrade one of your own settlements; you cannot place a city on an empty intersection.
  - Replace that settlement piece with a city piece from your supply (you have up to 4 cities). Return the settlement to your supply.
  - All normal placement distances are already satisfied because you’re upgrading an existing settlement.

- Effect:
  - That location is now worth 2 Victory Points instead of 1.
  - When an adjacent hex’s number is rolled (and the robber isn’t on it), you collect 2 resource cards of that hex’s type instead of 1.

Notes:
- You can build a settlement and then upgrade it to a city in the same turn if you can pay for both and have the pieces.
- You can’t upgrade if you’re out of city pieces.

Reference: Catan Base Game Rulebook, “Building: Cities” (cost and upgrade procedure) and “Producing Resources” (cities collect 2 cards).

It is significantly more complete, covering not just the cost and placement but also when you can build, the effects of a city (VP and resource production), and important notes. Despite being more comprehensive, it remains concise and well-organized, with every piece of information adding value for a player.

answers.question: What happens when I run out of roads?
answers.response:
You can’t build any more roads once you’ve placed all of your roads on the board. Catan limits you to the pieces in your personal supply (15 roads in the base game), and pieces are never moved or returned once placed.

- Rule reference: Build phase (Catan base game, 5th ed., p. 5–6): you may build only if you have the piece available in your supply; roads cannot be moved once placed.
- Road Building development card (p. 9): if you have fewer than 2 roads left, you may place only as many as you still have.

Running out of roads doesn’t affect roads already on the board, and it doesn’t prevent you from building settlements/cities that your existing network already reaches—you just can’t extend your network further until (in standard base Catan) you somehow have roads back, which essentially doesn’t happen.

answers.question: If I have multiple VP development cards, can I play them all in the same turn?
answers.response:
Yes. Victory Point development cards are exempt from the “one development card per turn” limit. You may reveal any number of VP cards on your turn, and you may reveal VP cards even on the same turn you bought them. Revealing them does not count as your one development card play for the turn.

Relevant rule: Catan base game rulebook (5th ed.), Development Cards section (p. 6–7): you may play only one development card per turn and not on the turn you buy it, “except for Victory Point cards,” which may be played immediately and in any number.

answers.question: How can the Robber get activated?
answers.response:
In the base game of Catan, the Robber is activated in exactly two ways:

- A 7 is rolled on the production dice. See “If You Roll a ‘7’” and “The Robber” sections: all players with more than 7 cards discard half (rounded down), then the roller must move the Robber to a different hex and steal 1 random resource from a player with a settlement/city adjacent to that hex.

- A Knight (formerly called Soldier) development card is played. See “Development Cards – Knight”: the player moves the Robber to a hex (it must change hexes) and steals 1 random resource from an adjacent player. No discards occur with a Knight—only with a rolled 7.

Notes:
- The Robber starts on the desert but is not “activated” until moved by one of the above.
- You may move the Robber onto any terrain hex (including the desert); it just can’t remain on the same hex it occupied.

answers.question: When does the game end?
answers.response:
Base Catan: The game ends immediately when a player reaches 10 victory points on their own turn; that player wins. You may reveal Victory Point development cards on your turn to claim the win, and gaining/claiming Longest Road or Largest Army on your turn can also push you to 10. You cannot win during another player’s turn, even if Longest Road/Largest Army changes hands then—you must wait until your turn. (Rulebook, “End of the Game,” e.g., 5th ed., p. 11.)

Notes:
- Victory Point development cards are kept hidden and only revealed on your turn; revealing them can end the game immediately on that turn.
- Some expansions/scenarios use different target totals (e.g., Cities & Knights: 13 VP; many Seafarers scenarios: scenario-specific).

answers.question: Can I "give away" resources with the following trade scheme? Player A trades 2 sheep for 1 wheat with player B. Then, Player A trades 1 wheat for 1 sheep with player B.
answers.response:
Short answer: Yes. Both of those trades are individually legal, so the sequence is legal.

Why: In Catan, during your turn you may conduct any number of domestic trades at mutually agreed rates, as long as both sides receive at least one resource card in each trade and there are no “future considerations” (no IOUs). See Rulebook, Trade with Other Players (Domestic Trade): you may trade any number of cards “at mutually agreeable terms,” but “you may not give away cards; both players must receive at least one resource card” and “no trade on credit.”

- Trade 1 (A gives 2 sheep for 1 wheat) is legal.
- Trade 2 (A gives 1 wheat for 1 sheep) is also legal.
- Net effect is effectively gifting 1 sheep, but the rules only forbid outright gifting within a single transaction; they do not prohibit multiple sequential trades that are unfavorable to one side.

Notes:
- Only the active player may conduct domestic trade, so this must all occur on Player A’s turn.
- Some groups/tournaments use stricter anti-collusion/anti-kingmaking rules and might disallow “disguised gifts.” That’s a house/tournament rule; the standard rulebook allows your sequence.

answers.question: How many VPs would this be: 4 settlements, 1 city and longest road?
answers.response:
8 Victory Points.

- Settlements: 4 × 1 VP = 4 VP
- City: 1 × 2 VP = 2 VP (a city replaces a settlement and is worth 2 total, not 1+2)
- Longest Road: 2 VP

Total: 4 + 2 + 2 = 8 VP.

Reference: Catan base game rulebook (5th ed.), “How Do You Win?” and “Special Cards – Longest Road.”

answers.question: How are ties broken if multiple people reach 10 VPs at the same time?
answers.response:
There are no ties in base Catan. The game ends the moment a player reaches 10 (or more) Victory Points on their own turn; that player wins immediately.

- Rule reference: End of the Game — “The first player to reach 10 Victory Points on their turn wins the game.” (Base Game rulebook; e.g., 5th ed., End of the Game section)
- VP development cards can only be revealed on your own turn, so only the active player can use them to reach 10.
- Longest Road/Largest Army can change hands during someone’s turn, but if another player happens to hit 10 due to changes on someone else’s turn, they do not win; only the active player can win when they reach 10 on their turn.

Some groups house-rule tie-breakers (e.g., fewest turns taken, most resources, etc.), but the official rules have no tie-break procedure—play is sequential and the first to 10 on their turn wins.

answers.question: When discarding cards for the Robber, do development cards count toward hand size?
answers.response:
No. Only resource cards count toward your hand size when a 7 is rolled.

Rule reference: In the base Catan rulebook (5th Ed.), “If you roll a 7” under The Robber states that each player with more than 7 resource cards must discard half (rounded down). Development cards are not resource cards and are not counted or discarded.

Timing note: The discard happens immediately after the 7 is rolled, before any development cards can be played, so you can’t play a card to change your hand size at that moment.

answers.question: Can new settlements be built along existing roads?
answers.response:
Short answer: You can’t place a settlement “on” a road segment (edges are for roads), but you can build a settlement on any empty intersection that is connected to your own road and obeys the distance rule—even if other players’ roads run into that intersection.

Key points (Catan base rules):
- Settlements go on intersections; roads go on edges. You cannot place a settlement on an edge/road segment.
- During the game, a new settlement must be:
  - On an empty intersection,
  - Connected to your own road, and
  - At least 2 road-lengths away from any settlement (the distance rule).
  (Rulebook: Building – Settlements)
- It’s legal to place that settlement at an intersection where another player’s road also connects; this will “break” their continuous road for Longest Road purposes. (Rulebook: Longest Road note — an opponent’s settlement on an open intersection interrupts a road.)
- Setup exception: During initial placement, settlements are placed first (any legal intersection per the distance rule), then you add one road adjacent to each. No prior road connection is needed for those initial settlements.

answers.question: What happens when the birds turmoil for the 4th time?
answers.response:
Nothing special happens. The Eyrie can Turmoil any number of times; the 4th is handled exactly like any other.

Per the Law of Root (Eyrie Dynasties — Turmoil):
- Immediately lose 5 victory points (not below 0).
- Discard all cards from your Decree except the two Loyal Viziers.
- Depose your current Leader and pick a different Leader (you may not select the one just deposed); move the Loyal Viziers to the two slots shown on the new Leader.
- Then proceed to Evening as normal.

There is no extra penalty or rule for the 4th Turmoil beyond the standard Turmoil steps.

answers.question: What are the types of Corvid plots?
answers.response:
Bomb, Extortion, Raid, and Snare.

These are the four types of face-down plot tokens used by the Corvid Conspiracy (see Root: The Underworld Expansion, Corvid Conspiracy faction rules/components).

answers.question: What is the difference between buildings, tokens, and units?
answers.response:
Short answer
- Warriors/units: The only pieces that move and initiate battle. They don’t use building slots. They count for ruling a clearing and can be removed in battle.
- Buildings: Stationary pieces placed in clearings, usually in open building slots. Many factions score VP when placing them, and some provide crafting. They count for ruling a clearing and can be removed in battle.
- Tokens: Stationary pieces that are not buildings (e.g., sympathy, plots, trade posts). They do not use building slots. Some provide crafting or other effects and may score when placed, depending on the faction. They count for ruling a clearing and can be removed in battle.

Notes and rules references
- The Law of Root (Definitions/Terms: Pieces—Warriors, Buildings, Tokens; and Rule a Clearing) defines three piece types and that all your pieces (warriors, buildings, tokens) count equally when determining rule of a clearing; in ties, no one rules.
- Only warriors move and initiate battle by default; buildings and tokens are placed and remain until removed (unless a faction ability says otherwise).
- Building slots limit buildings, not tokens. A faction’s rules may specify exceptions (e.g., certain buildings or tokens that craft, or buildings that do not use slots). Always follow the faction board icons to see which of your pieces are “crafting pieces.”

answers.question: If I strike another faction piece as the Vagabond, does that make my relationship hostile with that faction?
answers.response:
Short answer: Only if you remove a warrior.

Details:
- Per the Vagabond “Relationships” rules (Becoming Hostile), the Vagabond becomes Hostile toward a faction if he removes any of that faction’s warriors, whether by battle or any other effect.
- Removing only buildings or tokens does not make you Hostile.
- The Crossbow “Strike” specifically removes a warrior in your clearing; using it on a faction’s warrior will make you Hostile to that faction. If no warrior is removed (e.g., you only remove a building/token in battle or remove nothing), your relationship does not change.

Reference: Law of Root, Vagabond — Relationships (Becoming Hostile).

answers.question: When can an ambush card be used?
answers.response:
Short answer:
- An Ambush card can be played only by the defender at the very start of a battle, immediately after the attacker declares the battle and before any dice are rolled.
- It must match the clearing’s suit (Bird Ambush is wild). If played, the attacker may immediately cancel it by playing their own Ambush of the same suit (Bird counts).
- If not canceled, the defender deals 2 hits to the attacker, then the battle proceeds (and ends early if all attackers are removed).
- Only one Ambush may be used per battle (other than a cancel).

Notes and exceptions:
- You can Ambush even if you have no warriors in the clearing (defending buildings/tokens still allow it).
- Some crafted effects change this:
  - Scouting Party: You (and your allies) ignore Ambush cards played against you.
  - Other improvements (e.g., Armorers, Brutal Tactics, Sappers) don’t change when Ambush is played, only how hits resolve.
- Applies against any attacker, including the Vagabond.

Rules reference:
- Law of Root (Rules Reference), Battle—Ambush step (Ambush timing, suit requirement, cancellation, and effect).
- Learning to Play, Battle sequence summary (Ambush occurs before rolling dice).

answers.question: How does alliance revolt interact with the Lost City landmark?
answers.response:
Short answer: It doesn’t. Revolt only affects the clearing where it happens.

- Woodland Alliance – Revolt (Law of Root, Woodland Alliance Daylight): Choose a sympathetic clearing and “remove all enemy pieces in that clearing,” then remove the sympathy and place the matching base there. Revolt does not affect adjacent clearings.
- Lost City (Landmark Pack rules): The Lost City’s clearing is adjacent to all other clearings for purposes that check adjacency (e.g., movement, rule, some placement/spread checks). It does not change the clearing’s suit, nor does it expand an effect that targets “the clearing.”

So, if you Revolt in the Lost City’s clearing, you only remove enemy pieces in that clearing and then place the base matching that clearing’s printed suit. The Lost City’s global adjacency has no additional impact on Revolt.

Common misconception (acknowledged): Some players think Revolt might also clear adjacent clearings; that is incorrect—Revolt is not an area or adjacency-based effect.

answers.question: Which factions can lose Victory Points?
answers.response:
Short answer: Only the Eyrie Dynasties can lose Victory Points via their faction rules. Any faction can also lose VP if they use the crafted card Brutal Tactics (Exiles & Partisans deck).

Details:
- Eyrie Dynasties: When they go into Turmoil, they lose 3 VP, then reset their Decree (printed on the Eyrie player board; also in The Law of Root under Eyrie Dynasties – Turmoil).
- Brutal Tactics (crafted card): If you use its extra hit ability, you lose 1 VP at the end of your turn (card text; Exiles & Partisans deck). This applies to whichever faction crafted and used it.

Otherwise, Root scoring is cumulative—removing buildings, tokens, or bases does not make factions lose previously scored VP.

answers.question: Can the Riverfolk Company use the Tunnels card to move between trade posts?
answers.response:
Short answer: Only if they rule both clearings. Not just because they have trade posts there.

Why: The Tunnels card (persistent craft) lets you move between two clearings you rule, ignoring normal adjacency. Trade Posts are buildings and do not help you rule a clearing—rule is determined by warriors only (Law of Root, “Rule” definition in the core rules; Riverfolk Co. faction board also clarifies Trade Posts are buildings). So the Riverfolk can use Tunnels to move between two trade-post clearings only when they also have the most warriors in both those clearings.

If someone argues that buildings should count toward rule for Tunnels, that’s not how rule works in Root; the commonly accepted and rules-as-written interpretation is that only warriors determine rule.

answers.question: Can I purchase mercenaries and use them for the dominance victory condition on the same turn?
answers.response:
Short answer: No.

- Dominance wins are only checked at the start of your Birdsong (Law of Root, Dominance Cards: “you win if at the start of your Birdsong you meet the card’s condition”).
- You can only buy Riverfolk services (including Mercenaries) during your Daylight, and Mercenaries apply only “during battle as attacker” and last only until the start of the Riverfolk Company’s next turn (Riverfolk Company rules, Services: Mercenaries).
- Therefore, there is no timing window to buy Mercenaries and have them count for a same-turn dominance win—dominance isn’t checked in Daylight. By the time your next Birdsong occurs, the Mercenaries service will have expired (the Riverfolk will have taken a turn).
- Additionally, Mercenaries do not help you “rule” clearings for dominance; they only affect battles you initiate (“during battle as attacker”), not ruling.

Some players wonder if Mercenaries can help with ruling; the commonly accepted interpretation (and wording) is that they do not, since their effect is explicitly limited to battles you initiate.

answers.question: As the Scoundrel, can I Scorched Earth the clearing with the Marquise' Keep?
answers.response:
Short answer: No.

Why:
- Scorched Earth (Scoundrel ability) requires you to “remove all pieces in the clearing” before placing a Ruins token (Vagabond Pack – Scoundrel character board/ability text; Root Law: Vagabond—Scoundrel).
- The Marquise’s Keep token cannot be removed (Marquise de Cat rules—Keep; Root Law section covering Marquise de Cat Components/Keep).

Because you cannot remove all pieces from the Keep’s clearing (the Keep itself is non‑removable), the condition for Scorched Earth isn’t met, so you cannot use Scorched Earth there.

Notes on interpretation:
- Some players ask if you can remove everything except the Keep and still place the Ruins. Official/commonly accepted ruling is no: the ability’s “remove all pieces” clause must be satisfied, and the Keep’s immunity prevents that.

answers.question: If a player qualifies for multiple nobles, can they take all of them on their turn?
answers.response:
No. In Splendor you may claim only one Noble per turn.

Rule reference: Base game rulebook, End of Turn (p. 4). It states that at the end of your turn, if you meet the requirements of one or more Noble tiles, you must take one (and only one) Noble of your choice. Any other eligible Nobles remain in place for future turns.

answers.question: How do you win a noble?
answers.response:
You gain a Noble at the end of your turn if your permanent bonuses from development cards meet that Noble’s requirement. Gold/joker tokens and gem tokens do not count—only the bonus icons on cards.

- Timing: Check after you finish your action (buying a card or taking tokens).
- If you qualify for multiple Nobles, you must choose exactly one to take; you can’t refuse taking a Noble.
- A Noble tile is taken for free and is immediately worth 3 Prestige Points.

Rules reference: Splendor rulebook, “Nobles” and “End of Turn” sections (Nobles visit if you have the required bonuses; only one Noble may be claimed per turn; tokens don’t count toward Noble requirements).

answers.question: Can I take 2 gems of the same color if there are only 3 left?
answers.response:
No.

Per the core rules (Splendor rulebook, “Player’s Turn – Take Tokens,” p. 3): you may take 2 gems of the same color only if there are at least 4 tokens of that color in the supply before you take them. If only 3 remain, you cannot take 2 of that color (you could instead take up to 3 different colors, subject to availability). Gold/joker tokens are not part of this action.

answers.question: How many tokens are there in the game?
answers.response:
40 total tokens.

Components (Splendor base game rulebook, Components section):
- 7 emerald (green)
- 7 sapphire (blue)
- 7 ruby (red)
- 7 diamond (white)
- 7 onyx (black)
- 5 gold (joker)

Note: During setup you use fewer gem tokens at lower player counts (2 players: 4 of each gem color; 3 players: 5 of each; 4 players: all 7). Gold tokens (5) are always all used.

answers.question: Which cards can I buy on my turn?
answers.response:
Short answer: Exactly one development card—either any face-up card from the three rows in the center, or one of the development cards you have reserved in your hand.

Details and rules:
- On your turn you choose one action; one option is “purchase one development card” (Splendor rulebook, On Your Turn, p. 4).
- You may buy:
  - Any face-up development card from any of the three tiers on the table, or
  - Any development card you have reserved (in your hand), whether it was reserved face-up or face-down.
- You may not buy:
  - From the top of a deck (unless you previously reserved it).
  - Noble tiles (they’re not purchased; they’re claimed for free at the end of your turn if you meet the requirements; rulebook p. 5).
- You can buy only one card per turn.
- You must be able to pay its cost after applying permanent bonuses from cards you’ve already bought; gold tokens (jokers) can substitute any color (Costs and Bonuses, p. 5).
- You cannot combine actions, so you can’t reserve and then immediately buy in the same turn.

answers.question: If I cannot take 3 different colored gem tokens because there are only 2 colors left, each with less than 4 in stock, am I allowed to take just 2?
answers.response:
Yes. You may take up to 3 gem tokens of different colors as that action, and it’s permitted to take only 2 (or even 1) different-colored tokens instead of 3.

Relevant rule: Rulebook, Game Turn – Action 1 (“Take 3 gem tokens of different colors”). The official clarification/FAQ from Space Cowboys notes you may take fewer than 3 as long as they are all different colors. The “take 2 of the same color” action is only allowed if there are at least 4 tokens of that color in the supply, which doesn’t apply in your situation.

answers.question: Can I spend 4 gold tokens to buy a card that costs 4 gems?
answers.response:
Yes. Gold tokens are wild and can replace any gem colors when buying a development card, and you may use any number of them—even paying the entire cost in gold.

Reference: Splendor rulebook, “Buying a development card” (p. 4): you may “use one or more gold tokens (jokers) as substitutes for any colors” when paying a card’s cost. Note: gold cannot be used to meet Noble requirements.

answers.question: Do reserved cards need to be visible to all other players at the table?
answers.response:
Short answer: No. Reserved cards are kept hidden in your hand.

Relevant rule: In the “Reserve a development card and take a gold” action, the rulebook states that you “take [the card] into your hand” and that “a player may have no more than 3 reserved cards in his hand” (Splendor rulebook, p. 3). “In your hand” implies hidden information. Only the number of reserved cards you have is public.

Notes:
- If you reserve a face-up card from the table, everyone sees which card you took at the moment you take it, but you do not keep it visible afterward.
- If you reserve from a face-down deck, you draw it into your hand unseen by others.
- Any gold token you gain for reserving is, of course, public.

answers.question: What happens when a player cannot do any of the three required actions on their turn?
answers.response:
Short answer: In practice, this shouldn’t happen. If it somehow does, you simply do nothing and the turn passes.

Details:
- Splendor actually offers four possible actions on your turn (not three): 
  1) take 3 different gems, 2) take 2 of the same color (if ≥4 remain in that color), 3) reserve a card and take a gold (joker), and 4) purchase a development card (from the tableau or your hand). See rulebook, “Player’s turn: choose one action,” p. 3.
- It’s very hard to have no legal action because:
  - You may reserve from any face‑down deck if needed, even if there are no gold tokens left (you just don’t get a gold). Rulebook, “Reserve a development card,” note on gold availability (p. 5).
  - You’re allowed to take fewer than 3 different gems if you choose, provided you follow the token rules and end the turn with ≤10 tokens (discard down if over; token limit rule on p. 3).
- If a truly degenerate state occurs where you cannot take gems (none available in a legal combination), cannot reserve (already holding 3 reserved cards), and cannot purchase any card, the accepted ruling is that you pass (do nothing) and the next player takes their turn. There’s no additional penalty beyond discarding down to 10 if you happen to be over the limit at end of turn.

If you’re seeing this situation often, double‑check that you’re allowing:
- Reserving from a face‑down deck without taking gold when the gold supply is empty.
- Discarding down to 10 at end of turn (so you can still take tokens during your action even if it would temporarily put you over 10).

answers.question: When purchasing cards, can I choose to pay with gem tokens instead of cards or vice versa?
answers.response:
Short answer: No.

- Development cards are never “spent.” They give permanent bonuses (discounts) to future purchases and stay in front of you. You cannot pay with them. See Rulebook: Bonuses description and Buying a Development Card (typically p. 3–4): “These bonuses are permanent and allow the player to purchase development cards for a lesser cost.”
- To buy a card, you reduce its printed cost by your permanent bonuses, then pay the remaining cost using gem tokens (and/or gold jokers). If your bonuses cover the full cost, you pay 0 tokens.
- You also can’t choose to ignore your bonuses to “pay with tokens instead.” The discount is automatic; you must pay the exact remaining cost after discounts. This is the commonly accepted and official interpretation (Space Cowboys FAQ/Rulebook).

It provides excellent additional context and explanation, making it more complete.
